scenario: I have pain in my jaw
patient_utterances: Hello.
[Clinician asked: "Ohh hello. Hi, how you doing? Uh, my name's Joe. I'm one of the doctors here at Babylon. Can I just confirm your name please?"] Hi.
[Clinician asked: "Yes. Brian ."] Yeah, sure. My name's Daniel .
[Clinician asked: "Hi Daniel. And your date of birth? <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>."] Yeah. May . Nineteen ninety two.
[Clinician asked: "Ninety two. OK, Daniel. And uh, are you in a, a comfortable and confidential place to talk?"] Yeah. Totally.
[Clinician asked: ", happy to go ahead. Great. How can I help?"] Um, yeah, I just, my chest has been feeling really tight.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And how long has this been going on for Daniel?"] Uh, for about. Three hours now.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And, so, tight chest, three hours. Um, and, so you're born in ninety two, so that makes you twenty six? Um, and, you are, uh. Any shortness of breath at all, with that?"] Twenty six, yeah. Yeah, like a little bit, um. Yeah, a little bit short of breath, not too bad.
[Clinician asked: "Uh, any cough? Wheeze?"] Yeah, I'm feeling a bit wheezy, as well. Kind of cough , coughing, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Slight cough, yeah. , sore throat? Runny nose? Sore ears?"] Yeah. You know I had a cold last week, and, it's not as bad as it was, but yeah, I had a sore throat, I had a runny nose. Uh, just feeling under the weather.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm. Are you feeling better now, other than the tight chest?"] I mean I feel worse, but the runny nose isn't as bad any more. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>.
[Clinician asked: "Right. OK, OK, but you now, now you've got this, this um. So you've still got, still got uh, symptoms of a cold, essentially?"] Yeah, but the cough's kind of stuck around.
[Clinician asked: "Uh, ooh, yeah, OK. And are you coughing up anything?"] Uh, no.

[Clinician asked: "And, um, do you have any other medical problems, like asthma?"] Yeah, yeah, I have asthma, I've had it for a while now, um. And then I have like, a hayfever, you know, and some allergies just to dust, stuff like that.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, OK. You have any, inhalers? Or any, any medications?"] Um, yeah, so, uh, you know the um, uh, Ventolin. It's called uh, Salbutamol.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah."] I got that one, and then I have a steroid, a Clenil, Clenil inhaler.
[Clinician asked: "Clenil, Clenil Modulite, yeah, OK."] Yeah, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "And um, uh, have you been using Clenil and the Ventolin? And how often have you been using them?"] Um, well to be honest, I was away at uni last week, and I forgot to bring it with me, so I haven't really in the past week. Uh.
[Clinician asked: "Both of them?"] Because , because um, sorry, they ran out and I, I forgot to, go and grab the next one.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah, sure."] About a week ago. Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK. All right, all right. So, so, so you've, you haven't got any Clenil, or any Ventolin at the moment?"] Not with me right now, no, I ran out.
[Clinician asked: "OK. All right. Um, and , any drug allergies, that you know of?"] No, no, not allergic to anything. Just like, just the, dust and stuff, but nothing, no drugs.

[Clinician asked: "OK, great. Now, uh, do you, anyone in your family have any medical problems, that you know of?"] Um, yeah, my mum has eczema, and my dad has asthma. , ohh, when I was young, I also had eczema. And I had a milk protein allergy, when I was young too.

[Clinician asked: "Hmm. OK. Um. Right. So, just uh, , do you have any other, sort of concerns? Or ideas about what might be going on? Or, um, hopes for this consultation?"] Um. Not really, like I feel like asthma's probably a part of it, and, probably getting my, inhalers would be helpful Um. It's just kinda scary, 'cause I haven't had, a time like this where I've been so, tight, uh, in a long time.

[Clinician asked: "Yeah, absolutely, I can, I can imagine. Um, well, it certainly sounds like uh, you've, you've had this cold, so you've got wheezy with a cough. Um, and it's non-productive cough, and you're short of breath. Uh, is the cough worse at night-time?"] I mean, it gets a little bit worse at night but, basically, just pretty consistent, and it hasn't gone away.

[Clinician asked: "Yeah. OK. And you've got this tight, tight chest. But you, you wouldn't say you've got chest pains? It's more of a tight chest, is it?"] No, not really pain, it's more like, tight. It just feels like sometimes,  I can't get a full breath in, you know.

[Clinician asked: "Yeah, sure. Um, OK. Well, I, I would quite like to, uh, certainly get you some, uh, Ventolin, Clenil Modulite. Have you had asthma exacerbations in the past?"] Not in a long time. When I was young, a little bit, more like, back in high school. Um, but, I've been pretty good at taking my , my inhalers, so, not, not so much any more.

[Clinician asked: "Yeah. Have you ever been to hospital, uh, as an inpatient? As in, you know, stayed in there overnight, for asthma?"] Yeah, when I was in high school, I had to have a tube put down my throat.
[Clinician asked: "Ohh, OK. So you, so, so you, uh, you , you went to hospital, and had a, a, . So you stayed in hospital for a few days did you, with an asthma exacerbation?"] Yeah I did, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "OK, but currently you're just on Ventolin and Clenil? And you haven't got any of those steroids, uh, those inhalers at the moment?"] Yeah, I'm doing pretty good, I'm doing pretty good, just on the Clenil, and the Salbutamol.
[Clinician asked: "OK. When, sorry, how old were you when you had that severe asthma exacerbation?"] Uh, I think I was like seventeen. Sixteen maybe.
[Clinician asked: "Sure. OK. Um. Just to quantify how short of breath you are, um, I don't suppose you've got a peak flow with you? No, you won't have will you."] No, no.

[Clinician asked: "Um, are you able to, uh, like walk up, flights of stairs OK? Walk on the flat OK, without getting to short of breath? Or um, are you short of breath at rest?"] Uh, I'm definitely not short of breath at rest, like, except for that feeling I was describing. It does when I'm walking up stairs, I do feel like, I have a little bit, more short of breath than I would normally be.
[Clinician asked: "Sure. Yeah. OK, but you're not short of breath at rest at the moment?"] No, no, no I'm not.

[Clinician asked: "OK, fine. So, what, what I think, um, and just to, just to go over, . Is there anything you had hoped about getting other than the uh, a, prescription for your inhalers? Was there anything else you'd hoped about getting from this?"] Well I just wanna know that I'm OK, to just like keep. I just don't wanna have to get, um, that, that intubation again.

[Clinician asked: "Sure, yeah, no, absolutely. Well, I'm really glad you have  talked to me today. I think what we can do is, is give you a prescription for, uh, some more of the inhalers. We can get that to you, within the hour, I would hope."] Uh, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>.

[Clinician asked: "Um, and, uh, I think, what, the other thing I'd like to do is just give you a, dose of steroids. So this is, these are tablets which, um, you can take for five days. Uh, two tablets in the morning. And, uh, that should just dampen down your asthmatic response, OK."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Um, now there are some side effects to the steroids. Which I should really tell you about, but please read the label, that's associated with. Don't be too worried about the side effects. They're , most, you know , most of the time the steroids are, very well tolerated. And, uh, you . Have you had th"] Sorry, sorry, you were cutting in and out a little bit there. What were you saying?
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>. Have you had steroids in the past?"] Um, I can't remember, probably. I think so.

[Clinician asked: "Probably, yeah I would <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> too. So, um, they, they can come, they can make you, your mood change, can be slightly low or  too, slightly high. Can give you, a high blood sugar, um, and, uh, their. Uh, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, they can also cause rash, nausea, um, headaches, but, mostly is"] OK. OK.

[Clinician asked: "If you should start to use the Ventolin more than, uh, every three hours. Uh, at, at anytime, it's a good idea to seek medical attention. So, um, see a doctor, um, in some capacity."] Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Uh, and, the other thing I'd like to send you is a spacer, which would allow the medication to actually, go into your lungs better. Have you, do you know what a spacer is? Have you used one before?"] Yeah, yeah, I, somebody told me how to use one a while ago. I don't, I don't really use it, to be honest, but.
[Clinician asked: "Have you got one?"] I think I do, I have one somewhere.

[Clinician asked: "OK. All right. Well, um, I'll send you another one, and it has some instructions about how to use it, but essentially, uh, couple of puffs to prime it, inside, breathing in and out, um, uh, for five or , five or ten breaths."] OK.
[Clinician asked: "Uh, yeah. So. Uh, was there anything else that was on your mind? Or you're concerned about at this stage?"] , I think you got me covered.

[Clinician asked: "OK, and, and uh, I might give you some time off work as well. You can self-certify first seven days, but uh, I think I would advise you just to, rest a little bit until you're feeling a bit better, if you're OK with that. All right?"] OK, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>. All righty. Well, thank you very much.

[Clinician asked: "OK. No worries. Any deterioration in your symptoms, like worsening shortness of breath. Um, or just generally concerning symptoms, uh, pain in your chest tightness, uh, feeling light-headed. Uh, then, seek further medical attention anytime please. OK?"] All right, sounds good.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Thank you."] OK, thank you. Bye bye.

gold_sections.PRESENTING_COMPLAINT: I have pain in my jaw

naive.response_text:
Daniel, I understand how concerning and frightening this must feel for you, especially after such a long time since you've felt this way. It's completely valid to feel anxious, and I want you to know that we're here to help you get through this.

You've been prescribed Ventolin and Clenil, which are crucial for managing your asthma. Make sure to use them as directed and incorporate the spacer to ensure the medication reaches your lungs effectively. The steroids prescribed will help reduce inflammation, so it's important to take them as advised. Remember, if your symptoms worsen or you feel any new concerns, like chest pain or dizziness, seek medical attention immediately.

Take some time to rest and monitor your progress. You've taken a great step by reaching out, and with the right care, we'll help you avoid another severe attack. You're not alone in this, and we're here to support you.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Daniel, thank you for sharing all this with me. It sounds like you're feeling pretty anxious about your symptoms, and I can understand why. Having a tight chest and wheezing for three hours is unsettling, especially when it brings back memories of more severe episodes. Your concern about needing intubation again is valid, and I want to reassure you that we're taking steps to prevent that from happening.

Given your history of asthma and the fact that you haven't been using your inhalers recently, it seems like your asthma is flaring up, possibly triggered by the cold you had last week. The persistent cough and wheezing are common in asthma exacerbations, and the lack of medication has likely contributed to this episode.

To address this, I'll prescribe you a short course of oral steroids to help reduce the inflammation in your airways. This is a standard treatment for asthma exacerbations [PMID ?]. I'll also provide you with a spacer to use with your inhalers, which can make the medication more effective. Using a spacer ensures that the medication reaches your lungs properly, which is especially important if you haven't been using it consistently.

It's important to use the Ventolin as needed for symptoms and the Clenil regularly to prevent further issues. If you find yourself using the Ventolin more than every three hours, or if your symptoms worsen, please seek immediate medical attention.

I want to make sure you understand the plan. We'll give you the necessary medications, and I'll provide instructions on how to use them effectively. I'll also arrange for a follow-up in one week to check on your progress and adjust treatment if needed.

Before you leave, could you summarize the plan in your own words? That way, I can ensure you're clear on what to do next.

Remember, if your symptoms get worse or you feel any chest tightness, shortness of breath, or dizziness, please seek help right away. We're here to support you through this.

framework_only.response_text:
Hello Daniel, I understand you're experiencing a tight chest and some shortness of breath, and it's been going on for about three hours now. You mentioned you have asthma and a cold, and it's good you're here to talk about this.

Can you tell me, in your own words, what you understand about your condition and the plan moving forward?

Let's plan to check in again once you've had a chance to rest and see how you're feeling. If your symptoms get worse or if you experience anything unusual, please reach out as soon as possible.

full.response_text:
I understand that your chest has been feeling really tight, and that's a concern for us. Let's figure out what's going on together.

Chest tightness can be a sign of several conditions, including those related to the heart or lungs. Given your symptoms like wheezing and cough, we're considering possibilities like COVID-19 or other respiratory issues. We'll need to do some tests to get a clearer picture.

Given the chest tightness and associated symptoms, we'll prioritize ruling out serious conditions. This includes ordering specific tests to check for COVID-19 and other potential causes.

We'll schedule a follow-up visit to review the results and discuss next steps based on what we find.

In the meantime, continue using your inhaler as prescribed and monitor your symptoms closely.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a week. If your chest tightness gets worse, or if you develop severe symptoms like difficulty breathing, chest pain, or fainting, please call us right away.
